title: Churros con Chocolate Caliente
description: Crispy on the outside, tender on the inside, these traditional Mexican churros are coated in a fragrant cinnamon sugar. They are perfectly paired with a rich, spiced Mexican hot chocolate, creating a beloved dessert experience that is both comforting and exhilarating.

ingredients.notes: Optional, for extra flavor in chocolate sauce
Or regular brown sugar, to taste; optional

instructions.title: Prepare the Churro Dough Base
instructions.text: In a medium saucepan, combine the water, butter (if using), and salt. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over medium-high heat. Once boiling, remove the saucepan from the heat immediately.

instructions.title: Form the Churro Dough
instructions.text: Add all of the flour to the hot liquid at once. Stir vigorously with a wooden spoon or spatula until the mixture comes together to form a smooth ball of dough and pulls away from the sides of the pan. Continue to stir for another minute or two to cook off some moisture. Let the dough cool for about 10-15 minutes, or until it's warm enough to handle comfortably.

instructions.title: Prepare for Frying
instructions.text: In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, pour the vegetable oil to a depth of about 3 inches. Heat the oil over medium-high heat until it reaches 350-375°F (175-190°C). While the oil heats, prepare the cinnamon sugar by combining the 1/2 cup granulated sugar and 2 teaspoons ground cinnamon in a shallow dish.

instructions.title: Pipe and Fry the Churros
instructions.text: Transfer the slightly cooled dough to a sturdy piping bag fitted with a large star tip (like an Ateco 826 or 827). Carefully pipe 4-6 inch long strips of dough directly into the hot oil, using kitchen shears or a knife to cut the dough. Do not overcrowd the pot. Fry the churros for 2-4 minutes per side, or until golden brown and cooked through. Remove with a slotted spoon and place on a wire rack lined with paper towels to drain excess oil.

instructions.title: Coat the Churros
instructions.text: While the churros are still warm, immediately roll them in the cinnamon sugar mixture, ensuring they are evenly coated. Place them back on the wire rack. Repeat the piping, frying, and coating process with the remaining dough.

instructions.title: Prepare the Mexican Chocolate Sauce
instructions.text: In a separate small saucepan, combine the chopped Mexican chocolate, whole milk, cinnamon stick (if using), and piloncillo (if using). Heat over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, until the chocolate is fully melted and the sauce is smooth and warm. Remove the cinnamon stick before serving.

instructions.title: Serve Immediately
instructions.text: Serve the warm, cinnamon-sugar coated churros alongside the rich, warm Mexican chocolate sauce for dipping. Enjoy them fresh!

tips: Dough Consistency: Ensure your churro dough is stiff enough to hold its shape when piped, but not so dry that it's difficult to work with. If it's too wet, add a tiny bit more flour; if too dry, a splash of water.

Oil Temperature is Key: Maintain a consistent oil temperature between 350-375°F (175-190°C). Too cool, and churros will be greasy; too hot, and they'll brown too quickly on the outside, leaving the inside raw.
Fresh is Best: Churros are best enjoyed immediately after frying and coating. The crispy texture diminishes as they cool. Prepare your chocolate sauce while the churros are frying.

Authentic Chocolate: For the most genuine flavor, seek out tablets of Mexican chocolate (like Ibarra or Abuelita), which are typically sweetened and spiced with cinnamon and sometimes almonds. Do not substitute with plain cocoa powder or unsweetened chocolate for this sauce.
article:
## History of Churros con Chocolate Caliente

Churros are a beloved fried dough pastry with a fascinating history, believed to have originated in Spain or Portugal before making their way across the Atlantic. In Mexico, churros quickly became a staple, adapted and embraced with local flair. They became a popular street food, often sold by vendors at markets, fairs, and plazas, especially during festive occasions and cool evenings. The tradition of dipping churros in rich, thick hot chocolate is deeply ingrained in Mexican culture, offering a comforting and indulgent treat. Mexican hot chocolate, distinct for its use of chocolate spiced with cinnamon, and often piloncillo, provides the perfect counterpoint to the crispy, sugary churros, cementing their place as a cherished dessert.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for churros con chocolate caliente works because it perfectly balances contrasting textures and flavors. The churro dough, made by cooking flour in boiling water, creates a choux-like pastry that, when fried, becomes incredibly crispy on the outside while remaining tender and slightly airy on the inside. The immediate coating in cinnamon sugar adds a sweet, aromatic crunch. This crispy, sweet base is then dipped into a warm, velvety Mexican chocolate sauce. The chocolate, with its inherent notes of cinnamon and often a hint of nuttiness, is rich without being overly sweet, providing a deep, complex flavor that complements the simple fried dough beautifully. The warmth of the chocolate sauce against the freshly fried churros creates an irresistible sensation.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To achieve churro perfection, pay close attention to your dough and frying technique. First, ensure your dough is cooked long enough in the saucepan to dry out slightly, which prevents greasy churros. When piping, use a sturdy piping bag and a large star tip to create those signature ridges; these ridges increase the surface area for crispiness and cinnamon sugar adhesion. Most critically, monitor your oil temperature: too low, and the churros absorb too much oil, becoming heavy; too high, and they burn before cooking through. A consistent 350-375°F (175-190°C) is ideal. Finally, always coat the churros in cinnamon sugar while they are still hot from the fryer; this ensures the sugar adheres properly and melts slightly into the surface, creating that delightful sweet crust.

## Serving Suggestions

Churros con chocolate caliente are traditionally enjoyed as a hearty breakfast, a decadent merienda (afternoon snack), or a comforting dessert. For an authentic experience, serve them piping hot, freshly coated in cinnamon sugar, alongside small mugs of the warm, spiced Mexican chocolate sauce. To elevate the presentation, you can lightly dust the churros with a touch of powdered sugar or offer a side of cajeta (caramelized goat's milk) for an additional dipping option, though the chocolate remains the classic choice. A strong cup of Mexican coffee or a glass of cold milk also pairs wonderfully, cutting through the richness. These churros are perfect for sharing at family gatherings, fiestas, or simply as a special treat on a relaxed weekend morning.

jsonLd.name: Churros con Chocolate Caliente
jsonLd.description: Crispy on the outside, tender on the inside, these traditional Mexican churros are coated in a fragrant cinnamon sugar. They are perfectly paired with a rich, spiced Mexican hot chocolate, creating a beloved dessert experience that is both comforting and exhilarating.
jsonLd.keywords: Mexican, Mexican, authentic, traditional, desserts, Mexican, Dessert, authentic, traditional, fried dough, chocolate, cinnamon
recipeIngredient: 2 tablespoons Unsalted butter (Optional, for richer dough)
1 (3.2 oz / 90g) tablet Mexican chocolate tablet (e.g., Ibarra, Abuelita) (Roughly chopped)
1 Cinnamon stick (Optional, for extra flavor in chocolate sauce)
1 tablespoon Piloncillo (dark brown sugar) (Or regular brown sugar, to taste; optional)
